text: Objectives: Chlorophylls are antioxidants that are present in plant derived foods. Their role in protecting cells and organisms from damage caused by free radicals and reactive oxygen species is poorly studied, especially with regard to biological assays. Our objective was to assess the antioxidant activity of chlorophyll a and b as well as their Mg-free derivatives, pheophytin a and b and thus to assess the role of the magnesium in their antioxidant properties. We have used the comet assay, (assessing DNA damage by H2O2), the DPPH assay (assessing scavenging activity), iron chelation (assessing chelation of Fe (II) ions), as well as a biochemical assay for LDL oxidation (assessing inhibition of TBARS formation) during Cu-induced peroxidation. Results: Chlorophyll a and b as well as pheophytin a and b showed a dose-dependent antioxidant activity in all the tests and more so with pheophytins suggesting that the magnesium is not crucial for their antioxidant activity and can be even harmful to some extent. Therefore, chlorophylls and pheophytins act as strong antioxidants capable of preventing oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation by scavenging ROS as well as through the chelation of metal ions that are involved in the production of ROS.
